data.title: Forgot to set limits: One company burned through $500 million in Claude in one month.
data.description:
Author: Bao Yilong; Source: Wall Street News

The enterprise AI boom is encountering its first real billing crisis.

On May 28, according to Axios, citing an AI consultant, one of its enterprise clients recently spent $500 million on Claude in a single month simply because no cap was set on employee usage.

Analysts believe that many companies, in rapidly deploying AI tools, focus on functionality and promotion, neglecting the establishment of cost control mechanisms.

Tech giants such as Microsoft and Amazon are taking action to cut internal AI tools or halt projects tracking AI usage in order to curb excessive consumption behavior known as "tokenmaxxing."

An Amazon senior vice president had to warn employees: "Please don't use AI for the sake of using AI." The core issue facing the market now is no longer "whether to embrace AI," but rather "what have we gained from burning so much money?" Amazon's decision to shut down its ranking system reveals the real costs of internal "score manipulation." Amazon's case exposes the dilemma of corporate AI governance from another perspective. According to reports citing two sources familiar with the matter, Amazon's developer platform Kiro once had an internal ranking system called "Kirorank," which scored employees based on their AI usage activity. However, the ranking unexpectedly led to employees having AI agents perform meaningless tasks to improve their rankings, directly causing an increase in the company's computing power consumption. Amazon Senior Vice President Dave Treadwell admitted to employees this week that while the ranking's intentions were good, the end result was that employees were driving up the company's operating costs through "token maxing." He explicitly instructed employees not to focus on token consumption but to concentrate on building better products, emphasizing "don't use AI for the sake of using AI." Amazon subsequently confirmed in a statement that the beta dashboard "was not an official or approved tool and has been taken offline." A similar situation occurred with Meta, where employees also attempted to gain a favorable position in the company's internal rankings by increasing token consumption. This phenomenon suggests that when companies incorporate AI usage into their performance evaluations, it can backfire, distorting employee incentives into ineffective consumption of computing power. Amazon subsequently shifted to replacing token consumption with a "normalized deployment" metric, focusing on tracking whether engineers can consistently generate valuable code through AI. It's worth noting that Amazon's capital expenditure this year is projected to reach $200 billion, with the vast majority going towards AI and data center infrastructure. Four Major Challenges: Why AI Investment Isn't Yielding Returns According to Axios, enterprise AI adoption is facing four structural obstacles. Misaligned Use Case Selection. Sophia Velastegui, CEO of Velastegui Ventures and former Chief AI Officer at Microsoft, stated that most people tend to use AI to automate tasks they dislike, rather than the tasks most valuable to the company. She believes that companies should focus their AI resources on scenarios that directly drive revenue, rather than blindly deploying them. Lack of cost control. AI queries are not without cost; enterprise-level packages are charged per token, and even simple daily queries can quickly accumulate into considerable expenses, which most business departments are unaware of. Human resources are the biggest bottleneck. Velastegui characterizes the current common practice of companies "scattering" AI licensing as a path that cannot bring substantial returns. Companies pile a large number of AI tools on employees, but lack effective guidance and focus, resulting in low actual adoption efficiency. Concerns exist regarding data sharing. Josh Pantony, CEO of Boosted.ai, a company focusing on AI tools for the financial industry, points out that when companies are unwilling to open up their proprietary internal data to AI agents due to concerns about data security, the actual effectiveness of the agents will be greatly reduced, and the return on investment will naturally be out of the question. Token Economics: A New Core Variable in the AI ​​Narrative Behind this debate lies a more complex investment logic being reconstructed. According to Wall Street Insights, Rich Privorotsky, head of Goldman Sachs' One-Delta division, has recently stated that the core variable in AI trading has shifted from "whether the technology is feasible" to "whether the cost is affordable." DeepSeek reportedly lowered its token pricing by 75%, and Xiaomi MiMo's price reduction was close to 99%. This cost compression may trigger a "price war" logic similar to that following subsidy competition. He pointed out that infrastructure bottlenecks will eventually ease, and the market should not pay an excessive premium for "problems that are about to be solved." Rich Privorotsky further hypothesized that cheaper tokens might be the first to replace high-cost inference services. If there is a time lag in demand expansion, cloud service providers, model companies, and AI infrastructure revenue growth may face temporary pressure. He believes that the rationalization of token spending may become an important issue at the board level in the second and third quarters of this year, no less important than the AI ​​growth narrative itself. According to the Bloomberg Silicon Data LLM Token Expenditure Index, token prices have risen by about 65% since the end of February this year, while US AI software prices have increased by 20% to 37% cumulatively over the past year. This cost trend is prompting companies to re-examine their AI procurement strategies. As achieving 90% output at 10% cost becomes increasingly feasible, companies' reliance on high-cost cutting-edge models may systematically decrease. Ali Ansari, CEO of AI model training company Micro1, stated that companies are experiencing a "healthy swing" from overuse of AI to rational use. He believes that: Currently, the only truly effective area for AI is programming. The debate between bulls and bears: the same reality, two interpretations. Regarding the return on AI investment, the same data, analyzed under different frameworks, points to drastically different conclusions. From a bullish perspective, the current chaos is merely a normal growing pain in the transformation process. According to Goldman Sachs' Jim Schneider's assessment in early May, by 2030, agent-based AI will drive a 24-fold increase in token consumption, and gross margins for hyperscale cloud service providers and model providers will turn positive within the next 3 to 12 months. JPMorgan Chase's economic research also found that Python packages saw a jump in PyPI in early 2026, a trend that did not occur when ChatGPT launched in 2022, indicating that real productivity improvements are underway. A bearish perspective was systematically elaborated by Goldman Sachs semiconductor analyst Jim Covello in a report in April. He pointed out that almost all the value in the AI ​​supply chain has flowed to semiconductor companies, which is unprecedented and unsustainable in history. Chip companies should benefit when their customers benefit, but in this cycle, their prosperity has come at the expense of the entire upstream of the industry chain. Both narratives are unfolding simultaneously, and the outcome remains unclear. What is certain is that the simplistic equation of "increased token consumption equals successful AI transformation" has been broken. From extreme cases of burning through $500 million in a single month to Amazon's halt to ranking manipulation, AI investments are undergoing stricter scrutiny regarding their returns. The true verdict in this high-stakes gamble will be determined by how much real value the next AI bill generates.

data.title: From Casino Tool to Pricing Machine: Hyperliquid Through the Eyes of the NYSE
data.description:
Author: Zhou, ChainCatcher

Recently, Jeffrey Sprecher, founder and CEO of Intercontinental Exchange (ICE), the parent company of the New York Stock Exchange, attended the Bernstein investor conference.

When asked by the host about his views on the competitive threat posed by Hyperliquid, Sprecher revealed that he had personally met with the Hyperliquid team multiple times to discuss the overlap in their businesses and potential cooperation opportunities. He described the team as extremely intelligent and used the phrase "mutual admiration" to describe their exchanges.

At the same time, he posed a sharp question to regulators: If on-chain perpetual contracts are illegal, why hasn't Hyperliquid received a regulatory letter? If they are legal, why are traditional exchanges not allowed to participate? Why ban them from doing what has already happened globally?

These words carry significant weight.

The head of the world's largest traditional exchange questioned the double standards of regulators in front of investors, citing the legitimate existence of its competitors. This month, Bloomberg reported that ICE and the Chicago Mercantile Exchange (CME) jointly pressured the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) to investigate Hyperliquid. This was widely interpreted as a traditional giant joining forces to suppress a new force. Sprecher explicitly denied this narrative at the meeting. He stated that ICE was not panicked, but rather continued to communicate and learn from the Hyperliquid team. "We're helping them understand our world, and they're helping us understand theirs. In that sense, it's mutual admiration." The core of those conversations with regulators has always been asking whether ICE itself can enter the market, not blocking them. He bluntly stated, "If you think this is illegal, why haven't they received the same regulatory letter as us?" Looking at these two threads side-by-side, Sprecher's strategy becomes clear. Whether it's meeting privately to find out the other party's bottom line or lobbying regulators for entry, it's the same logic of paving the way, just at different levels of execution. In fact, ICE's plans are already accelerating. In March of this year, ICE invested in OKX at a valuation of $25 billion, acquiring a board seat; in the same month, it completed a $600 million cash investment in the prediction market platform Polymarket. Last week, ICE and OKX jointly launched perpetual futures contracts based on Brent and WTI crude oil, directly entering a market already dominated by Hyperliquid. Regarding potential future collaborations with Hyperliquid, Sprecher did not disclose details, only mentioning that the two companies are exploring areas of business overlap. However, judging from ICE's recent moves, the direction is relatively clear. The ICE-OKX collaboration model involves ICE providing benchmark pricing and the crypto platform handling execution. This division of labor also applies to Hyperliquid—ICE holds pricing power over the world's most important commodities and financial assets, while Hyperliquid possesses 24/7 on-chain infrastructure; the complementary potential between the two is not hard to imagine. Sprecher also specifically mentioned SpaceX's upcoming IPO. TradeXYZ, a platform built on Hyperliquid, has launched a pre-IPO perpetual contract for SpaceX. The contract does not require any SpaceX stock and is not authorized by the company. Its initial reference price was $150, corresponding to an implied valuation of approximately $1.78 trillion, and it briefly surged to $216. Sprecher stated that once SpaceX officially goes public on June 11th, everyone will see the difference between the on-chain pricing and the final IPO price. In his view, this will be a crucial moment to test on-chain price discovery capabilities, at which point regulators and market participants will be forced to declare that the price is either meaningless or extremely important. The Perp Identity Transformation: ICE's current sense of urgency stems not from the size of Hyperliquid itself, but from the fundamental identity transformation that perpetual contracts are undergoing. Crypto researcher @Pxstar_ calls this the industry's third moral liberation. Perpetual contracts, invented by BitMEX, were long considered the worst gambling tool in the cryptocurrency world. Exchanges profited handsomely from them, but the average user's lifespan was less than three months, with most losing everything within 100 days. Their liquidity didn't benefit the spot market; instead, it eroded it. The turning point came after Hyperliquid extended the underlying assets of its perpetual contracts to traditional assets like crude oil and pre-IPO stocks through HIP-3. The tool itself didn't change, but it began to serve genuine price discovery needs. From the beginning of this year to the end of March, tensions in the Middle East remained high, with many geopolitical decisions concentrated over the weekend. Traditional oil futures markets were closed during this period, and Hyperliquid's crude oil perpetual contracts filled this gap. Data shows that cumulative trading volume surged from $339 million to $7.3 billion in just a few weeks, an increase of more than 20 times; open interest also expanded from less than $200 million to over $1.26 billion. Sprecher points out that the platform gained such high attention precisely because many decisions were made over the weekend during the Middle East conflict. When a derivative product, once considered a gambling tool, begins to function as a price discovery tool in the world's most important commodity market, its nature has changed. This is why Sprecher remarked that this platform, built by an 11-person team and with an estimated gross profit margin of 99%, has created wealth comparable to Nasdaq. Delphi Digital also stated that perpetual contract exchanges will become the core hub of the digital value era, just as Wall Street was the core hub of the industrial era. Hyperliquid is currently the one that has gone the furthest. The Real Dilemma of Traditional Exchanges Faced with Hyperliquid's expansion, ICE attempted to address it by adjusting trading hours. Sprecher revealed that ICE communicated with several oil companies, proposing to remain open on weekends and operate in accordance with global trading time zones. The market reaction was lukewarm, but this outcome was not unexpected. Hyperliquid's advantage lies not in the extension of trading hours, but in the efficiency of its entire infrastructure. A fully on-chain order book, stablecoin settlement, completely transparent on-chain records, 24/7 operation, and extremely low marginal costs—this is a structural gap that traditional exchanges cannot replicate by adjusting their operating hours. More noteworthy is the situation of institutional clients. Sprecher mentioned that most of ICE's institutional clients are not currently trading on-chain, as internal compliance restrictions prevent them from participating at the institutional level. However, these institutions are closely watching, especially Hyperliquid's price discovery dynamics. More and more traders are already referring to on-chain price movements before traditional markets open. This means that ICE's institutional clients are in an awkward position: unable to directly participate in on-chain trading, yet unable to ignore the impact of on-chain prices on traditional markets. Regulatory uncertainty is also prevalent. The CFTC currently faces a fundamental question: what exactly is an on-chain perpetual contract? Is it an innovative product requiring a completely new legislative framework, or swap contracts that can be categorized under the existing Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act (Dodd-Frank) framework? Sprecher frankly admits he's unsure if regulators themselves have the answer. Meanwhile, both sides are vying for control over rule-making. Recently, Hyperliquid co-founder Jeff Yan traveled to Washington to meet with policymakers to discuss ways to bring the on-chain derivatives market under the US regulatory framework. Hyperliquid's policy center emphasizes that its on-chain transaction records are more transparent than any traditional exchange. In conclusion, Sprecher mentioned that if he were younger, he would also like to participate in Hyperliquid's endeavors. This statement not only acknowledges innovation but also reflects the complex attitude of traditional finance towards new trends. Traditional exchanges are gradually moving closer to this market through investment, partnerships, and regulatory lobbying. Hyperliquid, on the other hand, leverages transparency and efficiency to continuously expand its legitimacy. ICE understands this business; the next step is no longer whether to enter, but how to enter.

data.title: Historic Approval: CFTC Officially Allows BTC Perpetual Contracts
data.description:
By Mahe, Foresight News

On May 29, the U.S. Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) announced two landmark moves on the same day: formally approving the Bitcoin perpetual contract submitted by KalshiEX, LLC (Kalshi). In addition, the CFTC issued a letter to Coinbase stating it would not take enforcement action, allowing Coinbase to offer some perpetual futures products to U.S. customers through its subsidiary.

The CFTC issued a "Perpetual Contract Listing Policy Statement," providing a clear guidance framework for the listing of perpetual products in regulated markets. This combined action signifies a crucial step forward for U.S. crypto derivatives regulation from a long-standing gray area towards the compliant path of truly perpetual contracts.

Kalshi and Coinbase both receive regulatory approval. The CFTC review determined that Kalshi's Bitcoin perpetual contracts comply with the Commodity Exchange Act and the core principles of the Designated Contract Market (DCM), including the depth and liquidity of the underlying Bitcoin spot market, contract design, and risk management capabilities. The approval order requires Kalshi to continue operating in compliance and clarifies that the perpetual contract design "may not necessarily be applicable to all asset classes," encouraging other market participants to communicate with regulators and submit perpetual products for different underlying assets through the formal approval process. Furthermore, the CFTC's Market Participants Division issued an explanatory letter and a no-action letter to registered futures commission broker Coinbase Financial Markets (CFM), allowing it to offer Deribit-listed crypto options and perpetual contracts to U.S. users. The letter confirms that the aforementioned perpetual contracts can be classified as foreign futures under CFTC Rule 30.1. Under certain conditions, the CFTC will not recommend enforcement action against CFM for transferring customers' digital commodities and payment stablecoins to its overseas brokerage affiliates for margin purposes, whereby the affiliates may exercise reuse rights over the aforementioned customer assets. Previously, the US market lacked truly perpetual contracts (with no expiration date). Coinbase Derivatives launched a self-certified "perpetual-style" futures contract (with a contract term of up to 5 years) in July 2025, aiming to simulate the characteristics of a perpetual economy, but still retaining an expiration date. Today's approval and lack of enforcement action provide a dual compliance path for "true perpetuality": Kalshi follows the DCM standard futures route, while Coinbase reaches US customers through foreign futures + crypto collateral. In his statement, CFTC Chairman Mike Selig emphasized that perpetual contracts are an important risk management and price discovery tool in the global crypto asset market. Launching true perpetual contracts in the United States is a significant step towards making the US a global crypto hub. He pointed out that the CFTC has established a feasible regulatory framework for crypto asset perpetual contracts and will maintain market order by limiting excessive leverage, market volatility, and systemic risk. Selig also acknowledged that the CFTC's current regulatory stance has not yet formed formal, permanent rules, and future policies may still be adjusted as the regulatory environment changes. A trillion-dollar market opportunity. So why hasn't the CFTC approved true Bitcoin perpetual contracts before? Perpetual contracts are a "new" product within the traditional commodity futures framework. They have no expiration date and final settlement, which conflicts with the conventional understanding in the Commodity Exchange Act that traditional futures "must have an expiration date and a convergence mechanism." The CFTC internally discussed classifying them as futures or swaps, as different classifications would bring completely different regulatory requirements (including clearing, margin, reporting obligations, etc.). This ambiguity in legal status makes it difficult for platforms to obtain a stable compliance path. Furthermore, their high leverage and speculative nature, as well as concerns about market manipulation, have consistently led the CFTC to maintain a cautious approach. BTCPERP, as a perpetual contract tracking the spot price of Bitcoin, has no fixed expiration date. It settles periodically between long and short positions through a funding rate mechanism to maintain a close peg between the contract price and the spot price. In the global crypto derivatives market, perpetual contracts have long held absolute dominance. According to CoinGecko's 2025 annual report, the cumulative trading volume of crypto derivatives on centralized exchanges worldwide was approximately $85.7 trillion, with perpetual contracts accounting for about 78%. In 2025, the cumulative perpetual contracts on decentralized exchanges reached approximately $6.7 trillion (a year-on-year increase of 346%). Ironically, while Washington's regulatory traditionalists are embroiled in compliance debates, offshore decentralized perpetual platforms, represented by Hyperliquid, have already extended their reach to the S&P 500, crude oil, and gold through on-chain synthetic assets. As of the end of May 2026, Hyperliquid's perpetual trading volume had already reached $586.12 billion, and according to Allium on-chain data, Hyperliquid's total derivatives holdings also reached a historical high of nearly $60 billion at the end of May, with more and more individuals and institutions choosing to trade on it.

Hyperliquid Latest Holdings

This unprecedented approval by the CFTC is not only a compromise with the crypto market, but also a necessary "onshore defense" by the compliant world in the face of the innovative pressure from offshore "everything can be perpetual" assets.

In contrast, while regulated Bitcoin futures such as CME provide institutions with stable hedging tools, their leverage and trading characteristics differ from the perpetual products preferred by retail/professional traders.

This CFTC approval undoubtedly opens up a new battleground for Kalshi in the prediction market, blurring the lines between the prediction market and the traditional crypto derivatives market. Kalshi can leverage compliant event-settlement logic to tap into the perpetual liquidity pools that were previously held by centralized exchanges. For Coinbase, the trading volume and revenue of its perpetual contracts will likely be reflected in its next financial report. Previously, US traders primarily relied on offshore platforms, facing custody risks, regulatory uncertainty, and barriers to institutional entry. This regulatory policy, supporting crypto collateral, will attract traditional institutions such as hedge funds and family offices. Traders can hold leveraged positions long-term to hedge spot positions without frequent trading; it will also attract some offshore traffic back to compliant US channels. Meanwhile, the approval of Kalshi and Coinbase will stimulate the accelerated rollout of other products, such as ETH perpetual contracts, forming a more complete crypto derivatives matrix. In the long run, this policy may enhance the United States' competitiveness in the global crypto derivatives ecosystem, attracting more capital, talent, and infrastructure, and creating favorable conditions for the deep integration of crypto assets and traditional finance. Currently, this may be the most regulatory-friendly era for the crypto industry.

data.title: In the era of AI spending money for you—who's collecting taxes?
data.description:
Author: Prathik Desai; Translation: BitpushNews

In March of this year, OpenAI shut down a feature that allowed AI agents to make purchases on behalf of users. In the five months since its launch, fewer than 30 Shopify merchants had actually used it. The problem behind this was not inadequate payment infrastructure, but rather a lack of rules—at the time, there was no mechanism to ensure a seamless shopping experience. What could the agent actually buy? Who would withhold sales tax? How to prevent fraud? Who should handle returns and exchanges? These core questions were unresolved at the time.

Configuring a wallet for an agent, or building a payment infrastructure, is technically easy. However, enabling individuals or businesses to authorize agents to spend their money in a secure, trustworthy, and governed manner is extremely challenging.

Configuring a wallet for an agent, or building a payment infrastructure, is technically easy.

... Only programmability and clear rules can build a trustworthy ecosystem. This vast gap in the governance layer is harboring enormous business opportunities in the agentic economy. Last year, AI agents completed 176 million transactions globally, totaling $73 million. While this figure may seem insignificant now, McKinsey predicts that by 2030, the transaction volume mediated and facilitated by AI agents in global consumer commerce will surge to $3 trillion to $5 trillion. Therefore, companies building this new economy are currently racing to gain control of the governance layer—including controlling spending limits, identity verification, and compliance policy enforcement—thus deciding which agents can receive sufficient financial trust. Today, we'll delve into the question: Who is building the banking layer for these "bots"? And who will reap the astonishing rewards if they emerge victorious in this battle for dominance? Why pursue vertical expansion across the entire value chain? The underlying economics of processing AI agent payments is incredibly brutal. In the past 12 months, the average transaction amount for an AI agent was only 31 cents. Imagine how much profit a 31-cent micro-payment ultimately leaves for the institution leading the settlement after going through multiple layers of the transaction chain. If Stripe's standard fee model (2.9% + 30-cent fixed fee) is used, the profit left for the merchant will be less than one-tenth of a cent. Visa's interchange fee will further devour a third of that. In contrast, processing the same transaction via a Layer-2 stablecoin channel on a blockchain would cost only $0.0001. This extreme economic characteristic lays a solid foundation for the application of cryptocurrencies in the Settlement Layer. Currently, the payment infrastructure for the Settlement Layer is largely complete. Coinbase's x402 protocol handled the vast majority of the 176 million transactions last year, and approximately 3,900 merchants now accept smart agent payments. Meanwhile, Stripe and Tempo jointly developed a competing underlying standard—the Machine Payments Protocol (MPP), which officially launched in March of this year and has already been integrated into over 100 service systems. Furthermore, Google, Visa, and Mastercard also launched their own smart agent payment products around the same time. Within just 12 months, five competing payment architectures emerged in the market. However, the core pain point of smart agent payments is that relying solely on processing a 31-cent channel fee won't make anyone rich. Therefore, the real commercial value is highly concentrated in two core areas: the interest generated from fund deposits (Float, i.e., position interest), and the enforcement and control of smart agent payment rules (governance layer). In last week's article, we explained how companies can earn returns on deposited funds by controlling the "wallet layer" to lock up the stablecoin balance of AI smart agents. But this is only one of many layers of value that can be extracted. Another equally lucrative area is the "rule-making power" that controls how these idle funds are spent. These rules specifically include: spending limits, agent authentication, compliance policy enforcement, audit trails, and liability allocation in case of failed transactions. Currently, this governance layer is in a vacuum of fierce competition among various players. In April of this year, American Express launched "Agent Purchase Protection," an insurance product specifically designed to compensate for erroneous purchasing decisions made by AI agents. This move implicitly acknowledges that the current governance layer of AI agents is severely lacking in security. In this massive industry, which is projected to expand to $3 to $5 trillion in less than five years, whoever can fill the gap in governance defenses will reap enormous value. This is precisely why major giants are now fiercely competing for control of the governance layer. However, on what dimension should this governance layer be built? It could be a bank, a developer API, or even a wallet. Wallet: The Natural Gateway to Governance Every dollar spent by an agent must flow through a wallet. This makes wallets an excellent gateway for enforcing spending restrictions, identity checks, and manual approvals. Once you control the wallet, you control the governance. Third-party payment giant Stripe recognized this early on. In June 2025, Stripe acquired Privy, a cutting-edge company specializing in building "embedded wallets" for consumer-grade crypto applications. Through this acquisition, Stripe directly gained control of 75 million wallets spanning over 1,000 developer teams. Today, these wallets sit precisely at the most crucial "checkpoint"—before any funds are transferred, all policy compliance, limit capping, and manual authorization must pass through here. Furthermore, Stripe has quietly assembled a complete intelligent agent payment technology stack. It acquired Bridge to handle stablecoin scheduling and fiat currency exchange; simultaneously, it partnered with Paradigm to co-incubate Tempo, a Layer 1 blockchain focused on payments. As mentioned earlier, Stripe and Tempo jointly developed the Machine Payment Protocol (MPP), establishing open standards for how intelligent agents apply for, authorize, and settle funds. Thanks to this agent-oriented financial technology stack, the software system now allows AI agents to easily check balances, pay bills, deposit funds, open virtual cards, and make cross-border remittances. Intelligent agents can independently execute routine daily payments, but if any operation exceeds policy boundaries, the system automatically intercepts and escalates the request for human review. Currently, the balance in its treasury account is supported by Privy's non-custodial wallets, which are distributed across more than 150 markets worldwide. Even a giant like Amazon, when it needed to empower its platform developers to "make AI agents spend money," ultimately chose only two wallet companies—Privy and Coinbase. It didn't choose traditional banking giants or card organizations, but rather a wallet service provider that had only been established for five years. The underlying logic is that wallets are the ideal container for implementing point-of-sale controls; they can introduce just the right amount of human intervention, thereby ensuring necessary checks and balances and audits. In its industry report, "Who Pays the Agent," digital asset market maker Keyrock points out that the future commercial market for intelligent agents will "settle in a compromise—where agents will enjoy a high degree of autonomy, but must operate within 'hard boundaries' enforced by cryptographic technology, and where humans can audit or revoke permissions at any time." This is precisely the prime location Privy occupies within the Stripe ecosystem. A wallet is used to enforce boundaries on the actions of intelligent agents.

Governance Strategy for Smart Agent Wallets

In terms of specific governance implementation, Privy provides two operating modes for smart agent wallets:

Fully Autonomous Mode: The smart agent has absolute control over the wallet and executes transactions autonomously under the constraints of set compliance policies, without any human intervention. This mode is best suited for high-frequency trading bots and automated asset management smart agents.

Restricted Collaborative Mode: The ultimate ownership of assets still belongs to the human user, but the user grants the smart agent limited permissions, allowing it to act as a "signer" for the wallet.

Restricted Collaborative Mode: The ultimate ownership of assets still belongs to the human user, but the user grants the smart agent limited permissions, allowing it to act as a "signer" for the wallet.

Users retain the power of life and death, and can revoke the access rights of intelligent agents at any time in accordance with the law. Stripe's MPP protocol adopts a similar governance strategy. For high-frequency intelligent agent tasks, MPP innovatively introduces a "Sessions" mechanism. Under this mechanism, intelligent agents can be pre-authorized with a total consumption budget, and then continuously and uninterruptedly "stream" payments within that budget, without needing to repeatedly initiate manual applications for each specific transaction on the chain. Currently, MPP perfectly supports "sub-cent billing" for Large Language Model (LLM) inference and "precise billing per query" for data APIs. This extremely fine-grained governance is something that traditional bank card networks (such as Visa and Mastercard) simply cannot support at the underlying architecture level. Vertical Integration of the Business Landscape While Coinbase currently leads in total AI agent payments thanks to its x402 protocol, Privy possesses a trump card unrelated to encryption technology itself—the moat of Stripe's vast merchant distribution network. Coinbase currently has 3,900 partner merchants accepting agent payments. However, for every merchant Coinbase owns, Stripe's corresponding merchant base is nearly a thousand times larger. In February of this year, Privy officially stated that once Stripe merchants agree to integrate machine payments, the agent economy can achieve widespread adoption overnight through the existing Privy wallet network. Stripe merchants don't need to painstakingly build complex encryption infrastructure from scratch. As the battle between Stripe and Coinbase intensifies, traditional financial giants are not sitting idly by; they are accelerating the expansion of their vertical technology stacks through aggressive mergers and acquisitions. According to Keyrock's comprehensive map, the entire smart agent payment ecosystem is divided into six core layers: settlement layer, wallet layer, routing layer, protocol layer, governance layer, and application layer. Within this ecosystem, 179 industry projects are fiercely competing. In terms of market coverage, Coinbase and Stripe are the most dominant, each spanning five of the six tiers; stablecoin giant Circle follows closely behind, covering four tiers. In contrast, despite its massive scale, traditional tech giant Google has only reached two tiers so far, while payment giant Visa has only managed to hold onto a single, thin tier. Over the past 12 months, traditional giants have poured more than $8 billion into acquiring these technological gaps: Capital One acquired AI-native software platform Brex for a hefty $5.15 billion. Mastercard spent $1.8 billion to acquire crypto payment infrastructure BVNK. Looking at these capital moves, the "wallet layer" and "AI software layer" have undoubtedly become the most fiercely contested areas. Stripe acquired Privy, Fireblocks acquired Dynamic, and Arbitrum acquired ZeroDev. Without exception, all of these are examples of underlying payment infrastructure giants forcibly acquiring independent wallet providers. These massive transactions send a crystal-clear signal to the market: the industry has identified where the truly scarce assets lie. Pure, underlying settlement is becoming increasingly cheap and homogenized; conversely, the real high-value and profitable core battleground lies in how to manage permissions, control budgets, and clarify legal responsibilities through programming (i.e., governance). Simultaneously, this vertical integration generates a powerful compounding effect. Whoever can firmly control the wallet—the key to financial success—can naturally set spending rules, capture interest before funds flow, monopolize which merchants and applications receive "trust access," and extract substantial ecosystem service fees. The distribution effect currently occurring between Privy and Stripe is the best example. Looking at Coinbase's closed-loop ecosystem, the logic is exactly the same: every smart agent payment completed through the x402 protocol generates a continuous demand for USDC stablecoin on its self-developed Layer 2 network, Base. This directly contributes a steady stream of float revenue. These profits are then continuously reinvested in the development of its smart agent developer toolkit, AgentKit. AgentKit comes pre-installed with session limits, single-transaction lockouts, and safeguards that restrict fund transfers to whitelisted smart contracts. The more smart agents developed using AgentKit, the more x402 settlement transactions are attracted. It's a chain reaction, each layer feeding back into the next. The undercurrents of investment activity among industry giants go far beyond this. Coinbase Ventures, Coinbase's venture capital arm, recently made significant investments in three of the industry's most prominent independent governance startups: Catena Labs, Skyfire, and Payman. Circle co-founder Sean Neville personally founded Catena, and Circle itself has also strategically invested in Skyfire. Top Silicon Valley venture capital firm a16z led early-stage funding rounds for both companies. Even Visa, a traditional giant, has backed Payman and formed a strategic partnership with Skyfire. It's not hard to see that the same group of familiar faces who built the global payment and settlement infrastructure have now turned around and become major investors in the governance layer of intelligent agents. Their underlying calculation is quite simple: if governance functions remain an "embedded feature" within the existing infrastructure (as Privy plays in the Stripe technology stack), then these giants can maximize profits through the existing ecosystem; and if the governance layer eventually evolves into a completely new sub-sector, they can also make money passively through their vast investment portfolios. Just how big of a pie does acquiring the governance layer mean? Financial history has repeatedly proven that simply acting as a conduit and processing payment can never be the most profitable link in the industry chain. Because with the popularization of technology, financial infrastructure and pipelines will inevitably evolve into commoditized goods. Once commoditization occurs, the industry's excess profits will inevitably shift to the control hub—that is, to the decision-making level that determines whether a transaction is "permitted" and "under what preconditions it can occur." Throughout history, many traditional industries have fully traversed this painful path of "commoditization." Recall what happened when the widespread adoption of the internet completely shattered the profit-driven barriers of traditional cable television networks. All Internet Service Providers (ISPs, i.e., broadband operators) became indistinguishable and easily replaceable overnight. To avoid becoming mere conduits, telecom giants were forced to engage in frantic vertical expansion to protect themselves. In India, top telecom giants Jio and Airtel began cramming hundreds of TV news channels, half a dozen OTT video platform memberships, unlimited free calls, set-top boxes, and free routers into their most basic broadband packages. In the US, AT&T went so far as to acquire media giant Time Warner for a staggering $85 billion, transforming itself into a super-giant combining media content and telecommunications infrastructure. Its strategic intent was clear: it was essential to tightly bind Time Warner's top content assets, such as HBO, Warner Bros. Pictures, and CNN, with AT&T's own vast user distribution network. Only in this way could it withstand the disruptive impact of native streaming giants like Netflix and Amazon. When broadband network access—this most fundamental channel—becomes the least valuable appendage in the entire business package, value will frantically shift towards content, user relationships, and the ecosystem of products and services that can engage consumers. A similar scenario played out in the early days of the cryptocurrency world. Theoretically, settlement should be completed on the underlying protocol chain (for example, Ethereum uses a shared ledger for settlement across the entire network). However, when Coinbase launched its faster, low-fee Layer 2 network, Base, the company began legitimately extracting "Sequencer Revenue" from every transaction running on its own public chain. Today, Coinbase earns nearly $60 million annually just from processing transactions on the Base chain. Those currently building smart agent payment channels have clearly studied these lessons thoroughly. In previous articles, we explained that simply locking up the stablecoin balance held by a smart agent between transactions can generate substantial interest within the ecosystem. This gives any company that preemptively controls the "wallet layer" of the technology stack a massive cash cow. And once you further conquer the "governance layer," you unlock another, even more astonishing cash cow. Let's look at some data from traditional finance: Visa consistently extracts a 0.28% overall fee from its massive $14.2 trillion in annual payment transactions. This take rate includes not only simple hardware processing fees but also an extremely expensive "governance trust premium"—the trust premium that merchants and consumers are willing to pay, thanks to Visa's years of building a robust anti-fraud network, dispute resolution mechanisms, and strict enforcement of online rules. Even if we were to extract just a tiny fraction of this take rate and proportionally transfer it to the future world of intelligent business, the wealth it could unleash would be staggering. According to McKinsey's conservative forecast that the smart agent economy will reach $3 trillion by 2030, assuming the governance layer only charges a 0.1% fee (which is only about one-third of Visa's total fees), it could reap a net income of up to $3 billion annually. To give you a more intuitive idea of ​​this figure: Coinbase's total core subscription and service revenue in 2025 was only about $2.8 billion. This means that just from protection fees collected for helping people catch fraud, control transaction limits, and manage governance in smart agent transactions, its revenue could surpass the combined revenue of Coinbase's node staking, asset custody, and Coinbase One membership fees. In conclusion, the players who will truly reign supreme in the future will undoubtedly be those vertically integrated, full-stack giants who can simultaneously extend their reach into the wallet layer (earning interest on idle funds), the settlement layer (earning transaction sorting fees), and the governance layer (earning compliance and risk control service fees) within a single technology stack. In this surging era of intelligent agent finance, vertical integration of the entire technology stack will become the only ultimate business model for any company to maintain its long-term prosperity and avoid being ruthlessly swallowed by the tide of commoditization.

data.title: Mirror 1873: Understanding Today's AI Life-or-Death Situation Through the American Railroad Bubble
data.description:
Author: Nathan Montone; Source: M31 Capital; Translated by: BitpushNews

Historians often view the American "Gilded Age" as a story filled with bandit tycoons and peasant protests. However, investors should see its more practical essence: it is the clearest and most thoroughly studied example we have, perfectly demonstrating what happens when a nation's productive base surpasses the monetary system that prices it.

If you strip away the personal feuds and political drama of 1873-1896, what remains is a sophisticated machine of four parts, operating with predictable behavior and a definite endpoint.

Today, this machine is roaring back to life.

It is repeating itself in a mirror image—the direction of monetary policy errors is exactly the opposite of what happened back then—and, fueled by every increase in capital liquidity since the invention of the telegraph, the machine's operating cycle has been significantly compressed. In the first cycle, productivity manifested in the railroad, steel, and electrification industries, which expanded at a frenzied pace that the gold standard dollar refused to cooperate with. Prices fell continuously for two decades. Anyone holding debt was utterly crushed; while anyone holding this fragile currency reaped rich rewards even without doing anything. The ensuing protests (the Silver Faction, Blaine, and the famous "Gold Cross" speech) may have lost the election they sparked, but they ultimately won the long-term debate because the underlying systemic mismatch had to be addressed somehow. Seventeen years after Blaine's defeat, the country witnessed the birth of a central bank (the Federal Reserve). Today, at the heart of this productivity wave is computing power. The capital expenditure (Capex) data flowing from hyperscalers and sovereign AI projects is anything but ordinary; it represents infrastructure construction on a scale comparable to the construction of the U.S. railroad network in its heyday. And the currency priced in this productivity is behaving in the exact opposite way to gold in 1879. The dollar is not only not resisting expansion, but is being forced to expand at a rate exceeding the capacity of its institutional credibility to support a debt stack that can no longer accommodate real interest rates. The process of wealth stripping is also reversing: in the past, deflation ruthlessly crushed borrowers; now, suppressed real yields are ruthlessly reaping the benefits from savers. The channels of protest have shifted from the ballot box to the balance sheet. Sovereign institutions and organizations are quietly rotating towards Bitcoin and gold, without even needing to issue any political speeches. This article will dissect this four-part machine, pointing out three reasons why the analogy fails, and making three predictions with explicit conditions—if the conditions are not met, I will admit the predictions have failed. "Every monetary system follows the same trajectory: a slow betrayal of savers, a burst of populism, a reset." The key to understanding this historical analogy lies in the fact that the patterns of monetary failure in the two cycles are exactly opposites. In the first cycle, the dollar was too rigid; in the current cycle, it is too flexible. However, for those holding this dominant currency, the consequences in both directions are exactly the same: purchasing power is quietly flowing in a continuous and almost imperceptible way to holders of assets for which the current system cannot price honestly. In 1885, these assets were land and productive enterprises, which cash holders lost out to. In 2026, these assets will be sovereign-level scarce stores of value and claims to AI development (equity); cash holders will again lose out, but this time through inflation instead of deflation. 1. Productivity Boom 1873: The core engines were railroads, electrification, and the telegraph. The expansion of productive capacity far exceeded the limits that the monetary base could absorb. 2026: The core engines are computing power, AI infrastructure, and blockchain settlement, with sovereign powers vying to become the hosts of these developments. The approximately $700 billion in AI capital expenditure announced in the first quarter of this year represents a sovereign-level injection of productivity. These infrastructures will outlive the regimes that fund them, just like the railroads of yesteryear. The same is true of today's data centers. 2. A Fragile Monetary System 1873: This fragile system manifested itself in the maintenance of the pre-war gold standard, a fixed nominal anchor that could not be adjusted with the development of productivity. 2026: It manifests itself in a fiat dollar reserve system at the end of a cycle, under extreme fiscal expansion: federal debt exceeds 125% of GDP, term premiums continue to rise, and the Federal Reserve is virtually unable to implement substantial tightening without disrupting the arithmetic logic of fiscal policy. Today, the fragility has been reversed (manifested as excessive easing rather than excessive tightening), but the consequences are exactly the same: this system can no longer reasonably price its nominally measured productivity.

3. Deprivation of Savers' Wealth

1873: Its operating mechanism was deflation: nominal wages were compressed, real debt burdens continued to increase, and rural borrowers were utterly crushed.

2026: Its operating mechanism is real interest rate suppression: nominal yields are lower than real inflation, term premiums are compressed by official sector demand, and currencies continue to depreciate relative to productivity-anchored alternative assets. Although the signs of the mechanism have reversed, the result is the same: purchasing power is slowly shifting from holders of fragile currencies to holders of more robust assets. Today, those deprived of wealth are all those who save in dollars, and any country holding a currency it cannot issue itself.

4. The Populist Outbreak 1873: Blaine's Free Silver Movement was agricultural, indigenous, and organized through political conventions. It lost the 1896 election and subsequently dissipated as an electoral force. 2026: Today's outbreak is digital, global, and operates outside of electoral channels: manifested in sovereign nations' shift towards Bitcoin and gold, structured ETF buying, and a generational shift in assets that doesn't need a "Gold Cross" speech to mobilize. Focus more on capital flows, less on political news. Why the Protests Seem Calm: Blaine's movement was massive because those who lost money had no other choice. In 1894, a Kansas farmer couldn't open a Robinhood account and switch to Bitcoin; his only leverage was political leverage, which he pulled with all his might. Today's counterparts have exit channels. A central bank governor in a non-aligned country can add Bitcoin to their reserve portfolio with a board memo. A pension allocator can adjust duration without writing a column. The pressure that created Brian back then still exists; it simply flows through balance sheets, where it leaves no rhetoric. If you're looking for the 2026 "Golden Cross" speech, you'll find it on sovereign balance sheets, coded "BTC." Where the Analogy Fails: 2026 is not a simple repeat of 1873. Historical analogies are merely tools of thought, and like all such tools, they fail at the margins. There are three points of failure that deserve to be explicitly identified because they reshape the path of the future. In our view, these points of failure all point in the same direction—to make current predictions sharper, not more moderate. The cycle is no longer confined to a single country. The first version of this machine operated entirely within American politics. Congress passed the laws that triggered the cycle; American voters responded to it through elections; Congress ultimately passed laws to resolve the issue. From the Coin Act of 1873 to the establishment of the Federal Reserve, the entire arc was domestic. The current version is entirely different. The dollar's reserve role is now largely controlled by foreign central banks, not anyone in Washington. The asset rotation that has already begun is driven by allocation choices made by Beijing, Riyadh, Brasilia, and a dozen other capitals that owe nothing to the United States politically. There is no "Brian" to be defeated here, nor is there a Federal Reserve Act to wait for. The ultimate solution will not be legislation, but rather a slow, distributed evolution of who holds which assets. The timeframe has been significantly compressed. In the last cycle, it took a full forty years from the beginning to a systemic solution. This figure reflects a world where capital moved by steamships and information was transmitted by wire. In 1880, a sovereign nation wanting to reallocate its reserve assets had to physically transport gold; a farmer wanting to express preference between different monetary systems had to wait for the next election. Today, the friction in both places has been eliminated. ETF flows can be cleared overnight. Reserve managers can complete portfolio rotations before lunch. I expect the span of this structural cycle to shorten by about an order of magnitude: from 40 years to about 4 years. For anyone relying on a 19th- or even 20th-century timeline, the implications are unsettling: the window of opportunity between recognizing the cycle and missing out due to acting too late has collapsed. The current reset of assets is unprecedented. The most interesting "analogy" is actually the simplest. In the 1890s, silver was the asset this protest movement sought to monetize—a metal that had existed for centuries as part of bimetallism, mined in mines owned by centralized domestic interests, and stripped of its monetary status with a single congressional act (which it did). Silver was politically manipulated and captured in 1896, a treatment Bitcoin could never face. Seventeen years after its inception, Bitcoin has no parity to defend, and governments cannot sign any document removing it from the monetary discourse. This fundamentally changes the politics of adoption. Therefore, adoption will not arrive in the form of a black-and-white policy reversal, but rather manifest as a continuous accumulation curve, with ETF flows and sovereign holdings being the core explicit variables. "Today's cycle has been multipolar from the start. There is no Blaine, no Cleveland, only a global financial ecosystem." Future Predictions If this analogy holds true, it should be able to produce falsifiable predictions. Below, I have listed three predictions, each anchored to a turning point in the original sequence: the 1896 protests, the time elapsed between the protests and the resolution, and the institutional reconstruction of 1913. Each prediction is accompanied by the probability I give today, and the specific conditions under which I would determine its failure. These predictions will be entered into our standing ledger and reassessed quarterly. 1. The Next Two Years: A Modern Version of 1873-1896 The 19th-century explosion of populism was recorded at the electoral level. I predict the 21st-century explosion will be recorded at the sovereign reserve level. By the end of 2027, at least one more G20 sovereign nation, besides the United States, will formally announce Bitcoin as a strategic reserve asset. Probability: 70% Failure Condition: No new G20 sovereign Bitcoin holders are added by the end of 2028. 2. The Next Three Years: A Modern Version of 1897-1912 The original cycle took 40 years from demolition to the passage of the Federal Reserve Act. In today's world where capital flows are measured in milliseconds, I expect the cycle to shorten by an order of magnitude. By the end of 2029, there will be three or more new G20 sovereign states holding publicly declared strategic Bitcoin reserves, and the dollar's share in global central bank reserves will begin to decline measurably below current levels. Probability: 60% Failure Condition: For more than four consecutive years following the announcement of the first new sovereign state, Bitcoin adoption in the G20 stagnates with fewer than five holding countries. 3. The Next Five Years: A Modern Version of 1913 After Blaine's failure, structural pressures went underground and resurfaced in the Federal Reserve Act of 1913. I anticipate that the modern analogy will be resolved through institutional acceptance (compromise) rather than electoral rebellion: total sovereign holdings of Bitcoin, at the then-current price, exceed $200 billion, and the dollar's share of global reserves falls below 50% (currently around 58%). This 50% figure reflects uncertainty about the path, not the direction. I have a very high degree of certainty regarding the direction of this trend. Probability: 50% Failure Conditions: The dollar's share of reserves remains above 50% until 2030; or the coordinated restrictions imposed by the G7 on institutional Bitcoin custody prove effective in the long term; or a neutral reserve instrument issued by the Bank for International Settlements (BIS)/BRICS successfully absorbs defecting funds from sovereign states. Investment Implications: This analogy itself doesn't directly tell you what to buy. It tells you how to think about the choices that have long been present in every macroeconomic ledger. The direct implications are threefold: Cash is the key transaction now. The most crucial point to internalize is that in 2026, "risk-free assets" will play exactly the same role as long-term bonds in the early stages of the cycle's accumulation phase: this position will cause your purchasing power to quietly erode year after year, and this loss won't be reflected in any single account statement. Any portfolio that treats dollar cash as a neutral benchmark is essentially making an undisclosed gamble—betting that the end of this cycle will be different from the last. This gamble may pay off, but it is by no means without cost, nor is it neutral, and should be treated like any other bet, subject to position control and regular review. News headlines are just a distraction. If you only skim through the history of 1896, you might conclude that Blaine's failure ended the logic of silver investment. But looking at the longer term, the opposite is true: his failure merely shifted the protest from the political arena to a more covert, patient channel of institutional change, which is where it ultimately triumphed. The current phenomenon is very worthy of attention. When political opposition to Bitcoin or gold rotation reaches its peak, it's usually not the peak of trading. It's actually a signal that cheap channels of protest (tweeting, voting) are closing, while quiet, expensive channels (balance sheet adoption, company building) are opening. The sound of capital flows will grow louder, while the sound of rhetoric will grow quieter. Speed ​​Premium: Investors most prone to misjudging this cycle are those whose intuition is rooted in the post-1971 dollar system, which fluctuates slowly in units of business cycles. The 1873 cycle, after adjusting for the collapse of capital flow frictions, suggests this will be a much faster process. If I am correct—that this cycle compression is measured in "years" rather than "decades"—then those who assume patience in their positioning will only belatedly entering the market after most of the asset repricing process has ended. The premium will be reserved for those who are prepared for this cycle before the curve turns. Hard Assets: Bitcoin first, gold second. Bitcoin provides stronger explosive power (leverage) for the logic of cycle compression; while gold provides higher institutional acceptance for the logic of patience. **State Variables:** The cumulative holdings of Bitcoin by G20 sovereign nations. If you could only look at one chart in the macroeconomic ledger this year, this would be it. **Duration:** Long-term USD fixed income is the clearest expression of trading on the rigid currency side. You definitely don't want to be on this side. **Equities:** Any asset anchored to this productivity wave itself (AI infrastructure, computing power supply chains, the power and cooling stacks supporting it) directly captures the real economy portion of this cycle. **Patterns:** Recent forecasts are the first checkpoint. If they fail, I will publicly correct them, never quietly. The last time America's productivity surge exceeded its monetary capacity, the result was a crisis, a political struggle, a rise in populism, and a complete restructuring of the global monetary order. Today, this process is running again. The mechanism is reversed, the analogy fails in some places, but the dynamics are the same. Dollar savers are the disenfranchised class in the current cycle, just like rural debtors in the last cycle. Trading stands in opposition to this reality, and the window of opportunity to recognize this is likely to be much shorter than in the first historical iteration. Good luck.

data.title: Golden Web3.0 Daily Report | US Treasury Secretary Urges Progress on the Clarity Act
data.description:
DeFi data

1. Total market value of DeFi tokens: $64.164 billion

DeFi Total Market Cap Data Source: coingecko

2. Trading volume of decentralized exchanges in the past 24 hours: $43.59

Top 10 DeFi Projects by Locked Assets and Total Value Locked. Data Source: defillama

NFT Data

1. Total Market Value of NFTs: $7.341 billion

2.24 hours NFT trading volume 1.201 billion US dollars

Headlines

US Treasury Secretary Bessenter Urges Lawmakers to Advance the Clarity Act

On May 29, US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessenter urged lawmakers to advance the Clarity Act: "Our most important task right now is to bring the digital asset industry to the United States and make the United States the home of this field. I urge both the House and Senate to complete the legislation of the Clarity Act as soon as possible."

    AI Hot Topics

    1. Tencent Cloud Database Fully Upgraded for Agents, Providing Three Major AI Native Database Capabilities

    3. US AI Server Manufacturers' Stocks Surge Pre-Market: HP Rises Nearly 5%

    On May 29, following Dell's upward revision of its profit and revenue forecasts for the current fiscal year, stocks related to AI server manufacturers surged pre-market. HP (HPQ.US) rose nearly 5%, AMD (SMCI.US) rose over 10%, and IBM rose over 5%.

      4. AI Agent Security Platform Geordie Raises $30 Million in Series A Funding, Led by Balderton Capital

      On May 29th, Geordie, an AI Agent security platform, announced the completion of a $30 million Series A funding round, led by Balderton Capital, with participation from Crosspoint Capital, General Catalyst, and Ten Eleven Ventures. The company focuses on AI Agent governance and security, helping enterprises monitor the behavior, permissions, data access, and potential risks of internal AI agents in real time, and limiting non-deterministic AI agent decision-making behavior.

        4. AI Agent Security Platform Geordie Raises $30 Million in Series A Funding, Led by Balderton Capital, with participation from Crosspoint Capital, General Catalyst, and Ten Eleven Ventures.

        5. Waterdrop Capital CEO: Crypto Industry Improves Productivity, AI Transforms Production Relations

        On May 29th, Jademont, Founding Partner and CEO of Waterdrop Capital, posted on X, stating that at this point, the crypto industry is at its coldest, while the AI ​​industry is at its hottest. He wanted to tell some friends who were considering leaving the crypto space that these two industries—one improving productivity, the other transforming production relations—are the two legs of social progress, inseparable. Smart people can ride the wave, while ordinary people can wait patiently.

        ...

          DeFi Hot Topics

          1. NYSE Parent Company ICE Calls for Opening Up 24/7 On-Chain Perpetual Contracts to Promote Fair Regulatory Competition

          On May 29, the Intercontinental Exchange (ICE), the parent company of the New York Stock Exchange, called on regulators to allow regulated exchanges to launch 24/7 on-chain perpetual contract trading to achieve a "level playing field" with the native crypto market. ICE CEO Jeffrey Sprecher stated that regulators are currently restricting traditional exchanges from entering this area, while the relevant market already exists on-chain, and emphasized that the traditional financial system and the crypto market should be allowed to compete under the same rules. It is understood that ICE has already conducted multiple rounds of discussions with decentralized derivatives trading platforms on on-chain perpetual contract mechanisms to explore the synergy and potential integration paths between traditional finance and the crypto market.

          2. Aave V4 Total Deposits Surpass $100 Million

          On May 29th, according to the Aave Pro page, Aave V4 total deposits reached $104.9 million, with active loans reaching $30.69 million.

          3. Cactus Custody Integrates Lido V3 stVaults via Cactus Link

          On May 29th, institutional digital asset custody platform Cactus Custody now supports Lido V3 stVaults through its DeFi connector Cactus Link. Institutional clients can directly create and manage stVaults within their existing custody accounts, performing operations such as ETH deposits and withdrawals, stETH minting and burning, vault health monitoring, and rebalancing.

          ... This integration expands upon Cactus Custody's existing stETH and wstETH custody support, allowing institutional clients to now use Lido liquidity-staking tokens in conjunction with stVault operations.

          4. Sei Releases Giga Upgrade Roadmap, Aiming for 200,000 TPS and 400ms Finality

          On May 29th, Sei released its Giga upgrade roadmap. Giga is Sei's next-generation protocol upgrade, designed to achieve higher transaction performance by refactoring the execution client, state management, and consensus mechanisms.

          According to the roadmap, Sei will launch several core upgrades, including: Ares execution client upgrade (significantly improving performance through pipelined execution, asynchronous processing, and other optimizations); Eidos state management upgrade (replacing Merkle trees with flat key-value stores, separating hot states from cold archives); Autobahn consensus (aiming for 200,000 TPS and 400ms finality, already achieving 5 Gigagas processing capacity in an internal distributed testnet); Sedna private memory pool (introducing a cryptographic transaction propagation mechanism); and a gradual transition to an EVM-only architecture (deprecating the Cosmos SDK and CosmWasm through SIP-3). On May 29th, Coinbase announced the expansion of its crypto Travel Rule compliant network TRUST and the launch of the new platform TRUSThub, designed to help virtual asset service providers (VASPs) worldwide achieve privacy-first, cross-platform compliant information exchange without establishing centralized sensitive data storage. The official statement from TRUSThub indicates that, based on TRUST's peer-to-peer architecture, it allows members to share compliance data with global trading counterparts even when using different Travel Rule tools. This resolves the "sunrise period" compatibility issues that arise during regulatory implementation across different jurisdictions and lowers the barrier to entry for VASPs. Coinbase also announced the continued expansion of the TRUST network, now covering multiple markets including the EU, UK, Australia, India, Brazil, US, Canada, Hong Kong, Japan, Switzerland, and the UAE. **Disclaimer:** Jinse Finance, as a blockchain information platform, provides articles for informational purposes only and does not constitute actual investment advice. Please establish sound investment principles and be sure to enhance your risk awareness.

data.title: How can we enable the AI ​​Agent to call API ERC-8257 itself?
data.description:
Author: Shirley Li, Researcher at Web3Caff Research

Compared to human users, the biggest advantage of AI agents lies in their stronger autonomous execution capabilities under ideal conditions: they can complete tasks and perform operations independently, and proactively invoke external tools without continuous human intervention. However, in the actual process of AI agents invoking tools (such as trading platform APIs, data analysis tools, and oracles), some problems still arise.

First, the access points to these tools are scattered across GitHub, official websites, centralized API platforms, etc., lacking a unified discovery channel. Without human intervention, AI agents find it difficult to autonomously locate and access the required tools, and the specific payment methods of different platforms also vary, lacking standardized processes. This causes some difficulties for AI agents invoking tools.

Second, in the traditional internet, calling APIs usually requires developers to register an account, obtain an API key, and undergo permission verification according to specific rules.

This process was originally designed for human participants, but for AI agents, there is currently a lack of open and standardized implementation solutions for automatically completing registration, obtaining credentials, and invoking tools. While the x402 protocol currently supports AI agents in automatically completing payments, it is mainly applicable to open interfaces with a "pay-to-use" model and struggles to handle more complex permission scenarios, such as only subscribed users being able to access services, or only users holding certain credentials being able to enjoy discounts. To fill this gap, OpenSea recently launched a draft ERC-8257 standard, aiming to establish an open, permissionless on-chain tool catalog for AI agents, allowing them to autonomously discover tools, understand access rules, and automatically complete invocation and payment upon meeting the conditions. In short, the core of ERC-8257 is an on-chain tool registry. This registry is essentially a smart contract, allowing tool developers to register their tool's information and access permissions on the blockchain and make them publicly available. However, due to the high cost of directly uploading all data to the blockchain, ERC-8257 allows developers to store more detailed tool information on their own servers or domains, presented as a JSON-formatted manifest. The on-chain registry only records a link to this file. This off-chain file typically includes: tool name, function description, API interface, calling method, pricing information, payment protocol, access rules, etc. The on-chain registry needs to record key data such as the address of the off-chain file, the file hash value, and tool developer information. This design aims to prevent developers from subsequently tampering with the tool content. When the AI ​​Agent calls the tool, it can verify whether the off-chain content matches the information registered on the blockchain by checking the file hash value. Another crucial design feature of ERC-8257 is that access permissions are not fixed in format but defined through a separate smart contract. Tool developers can freely define this contract to specify who is qualified to call their tools. For example, developers can check whether the AI ​​Agent holds a certain NFT, a certain token, has subscribed, or is on a whitelist, etc. Consider this example: a certain on-chain analytics tool stipulates that the cost for a regular user to call a regular API is $0.05 per call, while a user holding a certain NFT only needs to pay $0.01 per call. Furthermore, if a user subscribes to its service (paying continuously via a specified token or payment protocol), they also gain access to advanced analytics interfaces. In this scenario, "holding a certain NFT" and "subscribing to a service" are two special access credentials. If the AI ​​Agent currently does not have the corresponding permissions, it can acquire these qualifications on-chain or in the marketplace (e.g., by purchasing an NFT or completing a subscription) and then reapply for the access. However, it's important to note that when access permissions exist in the form of assets such as NFTs or tokens, these assets themselves may enter the market circulation system, thus being affected by supply and demand, leading to high value fluctuations or speculative behavior. Therefore, ERC-8257 does not restrict the permission system to a single asset model, but chooses to maintain openness. Tool or service developers can choose different access mechanisms according to their specific needs. For example, they can introduce non-transferable Soulbound NFTs to avoid value fluctuations caused by transactions, or introduce non-asset-based mechanisms such as reputation scoring to reduce the impact of speculative behavior. At the payment level, ERC-8257 does not define the specific payment logic, but only requires developers to declare which payment protocols they support in the JSON file, such as x402, on-chain ERC-20 payments, or other machine payment protocols. The actual payment execution will be handled by the corresponding protocol. From an overall process perspective, ERC-8257 works roughly as follows: Tool developers deploy tool services and write corresponding access permissions, then submit the relevant information to the on-chain registry; When an AI Agent needs to call a tool or service, it can scan the on-chain registry. If it finds a tool or service that meets its needs, it can further read the detailed description file to understand the calling rules; If the AI ​​Agent does not meet the access conditions, it can try to obtain the corresponding permissions and then initiate the call again; Ultimately, the AI ​​Agent can autonomously complete the entire process of tool discovery, permission verification, payment, and invocation without human intervention.

Image source:The App Store for Agent Tools: ERC-8257

Overall, ERC-8257 attempts to solve not only the problem of how to put APIs on the blockchain, but also how AI agents can automatically discover tools, understand access rules, obtain access permissions, and call these tools in a standardized way, just like human users. From a design perspective, ERC-8257, together with the x402 protocol, will form a complementary relationship: ERC-8257 aims to enable AI Agents to discover tools globally and determine their access rights based on rules; the x402 protocol handles payment and settlement during tool invocation. Once a tool is authorized for use, AI Agents can be paid per use or based on invocation frequency. However, besides the aforementioned potential for value fluctuations and speculative risks if access rights exist in the form of assets such as NFTs or tokens, the ERC-8257 standard will face some potential risks and challenges in its practical implementation. For example, although ERC-8257 provides a standardized framework for tool registration and access, different developers still differ in setting access conditions. While AI Agents can rely on a unified on-chain index path for tool discovery, they still need to be compatible with different permission judgment logics during actual invocation, which introduces certain technical complexities. Furthermore, regarding trust mechanisms, AI Agents currently verify whether files have been tampered with during transmission by comparing the hash value recorded on-chain with the off-chain tool description file. However, this mechanism only solves the problem of data consistency and cannot further guarantee the correctness of the tool's operating logic, the trustworthiness of its interface, or the potential risks of information leakage during data processing. At the same time, since tool services are usually deployed on off-chain infrastructure, their long-term availability and stability still depend on the developer's operational capabilities. This means that AI Agents also need to rely on external reputation mechanisms for verification. Therefore, before the ERC-8257 standard is practically applied, its aspects regarding tool trustworthiness and the consistency of permission rules still need further verification and improvement.
